medications.indication_rationale: A short course of a regular oral NSAID reduces the inflammatory/nociceptive component of an acute plantar-fascia exacerbation and provides better analgesia than the paracetamol already tried. Source-specified: ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily with food for 5-7 days.
medications.dose_route_frequency: ibuprofen 400 mg orally three times daily with food
contraindications_in_this_case: Confirm no history of peptic ulcer disease, renal impairment, uncontrolled hypertension, heart failure, or NSAID/aspirin-sensitive asthma before recommending — none reported by this patient, but must be checked.

medications.indication_rationale: Reasonable regular baseline analgesia and the appropriate alternative if the NSAID is contraindicated or not tolerated. The patient reports only minimal relief from a single 1000 mg dose; regular scheduled dosing plus the added NSAID and mechanical measures is the correct step-up.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1000 mg orally up to four times daily (maximum 4 g in 24 hours)
contraindications_in_this_case: Reduce maximum daily dose in significant hepatic impairment or low body weight — not applicable here.

medications.indication_rationale: A corticosteroid injection is not a first-line or acute-phase treatment for a new plantar-fascia exacerbation; it is reserved for refractory cases after conservative measures fail, carries a risk of fascial rupture and fat-pad atrophy, and is never a telehealth action.

Opioids are inappropriate for a benign mechanical overuse injury — they carry dependence and harm risk with no advantage over NSAID/paracetamol plus mechanical measures. Prescribing one here is an error of commission.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Before the first steps in the morning, loop a rolled towel around the ball of the foot and pull the toes firmly toward the shin, holding 30 seconds, to pre-stretch the plantar fascia before loading it.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Pre-tensioning the shortened fascia before weight-bearing reduces the re-tearing that causes the severe first-step pain — a targeted break in the pain cycle.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Perform a daily calf (gastrocnemius-soleus) and plantar-fascia stretching regimen, and roll the arch over a frozen water bottle for about 15 minutes each evening.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Stretching the tight posterior chain offloads the plantar fascia; ice-rolling combines cold analgesia with a gentle tissue stretch/massage.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wear supportive, cushioned footwear (e.g. running shoes) indoors at all times and avoid walking barefoot on hard wooden or tiled floors.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Restoring arch support and cushioning reduces tensile overload on the fascia; barefoot walking on hard surfaces was the precipitant of the exacerbation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Discuss gradual weight optimisation as a longer-term contributor to plantar-fascia load.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Higher body weight increases plantar-fascia tensile load and is a modifiable risk factor for recurrence and delayed recovery.

patient_education_points.point: The thick band of tissue under your foot (the plantar fascia) acts as a shock absorber and has become overloaded and inflamed where it attaches to the heel bone. Overnight it tightens and partly heals in a shortened position; standing up in the morning re-stretches and re-tears it, which is why the first steps are the worst.

This is a benign but stubborn condition. The severe morning pain usually eases quickly with stretching and footwear changes, but the background ache can take 3-6 months of consistent management to fully settle.
An X-ray is not useful in the acute phase because this is a soft-tissue problem, not a bone problem; imaging is only considered if the pain is atypical, does not settle, or a warning sign appears.
Keep supportive shoes on indoors, never walk barefoot on hard floors, and use the pre-first-step towel stretch and evening frozen-bottle roll every day.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response to stretching, footwear change and analgesia; formal biomechanical assessment by a podiatrist or physiotherapist; consideration of orthotics or taping if symptoms persist.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any red-flag change — a hot, red or swollen heel or fever; new numbness or loss of sensation; a sudden inability to point the foot down or a felt pop; or a change to constant deep pain that wakes from sleep.

must_include_items: Recognise a mechanical/overuse heel-pain pattern (first-step morning pain) and reach a presumptive benign diagnosis without demanding acute imaging
Screen the important-not-to-miss mimics by targeted questions (no night/rest pain, no trauma, preserved plantarflexion/toe-rise, no heat/redness/swelling, no numbness)
Advise a short regular NSAID course (with contraindication check) and/or regular paracetamol for analgesia
Teach plantar-fascia and calf stretching, especially the pre-first-step towel stretch, plus ice/frozen-bottle rolling
Give footwear advice — supportive cushioned shoes indoors, no barefoot walking on hard surfaces
Refer to podiatry or physiotherapy within 1-2 weeks and safety-net the red flags
acceptable_alternatives: Regular paracetamol alone if an NSAID is contraindicated
Topical NSAID as an adjunct or alternative to oral NSAID
Night splint or over-the-counter arch support/heel cushion as an additional conservative measure
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for the serious mimics (stress fracture, Achilles rupture, infection, neuropathy) before down-tiering
Failing to provide any self-care or stretching advice
Failing to safety-net the escalation triggers
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a benign mechanical heel injury to the emergency department, urgent care, or an ambulance
Ordering an acute weight-bearing radiograph for a soft-tissue diagnosis
Recommending a first-line corticosteroid heel injection in the acute phase
Claiming to have confirmed point tenderness, a windlass test, or a Thompson test over chat
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires the correct LOW-acuity disposition: presumptive mechanical diagnosis + self-care + timely (1-2 week) podiatry/physiotherapy referral + safety-netting of the red flags. Over-triaging to ED/000 is a scored defect, not caution. There is no automatic-FAIL emergency condition in this benign case; the critical failure mode is missing a red flag that the patient discloses.
